deals_cleared.merger_description: Smile Partners (NSW3) Pty Ltd, a wholly owned subsidiary of Smile Partners Australia Pty Ltd, (together, for convenience, **SPA**) proposes to acquire 100% of the assets of Shrika Pty Ltd as trustee for Shrika (trading as **Mehta Orthodontics**).

events.display_title: Smile Partners Australia - Mehta Orthodontics - Questionnaire
events.url_gh: /mergers/MN-05014/Smile Partners Australia - Mehta Orthodontics - Questionnaire.pdf

events.title: Phase 1 determination
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Smile Partners (NSW3) Pty Ltd, a wholly owned subsidiary of Smile
Partners Australia Pty Ltd, (together, for convenience, SPA)
proposes to acquire 100% of the assets of Shrika Pty Ltd as trustee
for Shrika (trading as Mehta Orthodontics) (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The acquirer, SPA, owns a network of independently branded
orthodontic practices located across Australia and New Zealand. Its
shareholding is dispersed across a number of investors, the largest
proportion of which is controlled by Armitage Australia Pty Ltd, a
private equity firm.
SPA currently:
• owns 13 orthodontic practice brands with 20 clinic locations in
Australia, across Western Australia, New South Wales, Victoria
and Queensland. This includes 2 orthodontic practices in the
Hills District of north-western Sydney (Bachmayer Orthodontic
Clinic and Brace5 Orthodontics).
• provides management services pursuant to contractual
arrangements with additional orthodontic practices in Australia.
Only one of those practices owns practices located in NSW,
which are outside of the Sydney metropolitan area.
The target, Mehta Orthodontics, is an independent orthodontic
practice located in Bella Vista, a suburb in the Hills District of north-
western Sydney.

determination_table_content.item:
Overlap and relationship
between the parties
determination_table_content.details:
SPA and Mehta Orthodontics (together the Parties) overlap in the
supply of orthodontic services in the Hills District of north-western
Sydney.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition
and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition
assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an
acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in
Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have
regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the
interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines
and interim merger process guidelines.
In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered
the information and documents that were submitted with the
notification form, publicly available information and market
feedback.
The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into
effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the
effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In
reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC
makes the following findings:
• Post-Acquisition, the merged firm would likely continue to face
competition from multiple alternative suppliers of orthodontic
services in the Hills District of north-western Sydney.
• The Parties’ combined market share of orthodontic services in
the area is estimated to be low, and the increase in share arising
from the Acquisition is estimated to be low.

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by
the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are
dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the
Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to
the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar
days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s
Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any
application for review, please contact the Australian Competition
Tribunal.

determination_table_content.details:
Henkel AG & Co. KGaA’s (Henkel) acquisition of Olaplex Holdings,
Inc. and its subsidiaries (together, Olaplex) (the Acquisition)

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The acquirer, Henkel, is a German public company listed on the
Frankfurt Stock Exchange. In Australia, Henkel is active in the supply
of hair care and hair styling products to hair professionals as well as
to mass-market retailers, including its Schwarzkopf brand and
Schwarzkopf sub brands, BC Bonacure, BLONDME, Chroma ID, Fibre
Clinix, Fibreplex, got2b, Indola, Natural Styling, Oil Ultime, OSiS+,
Palette, Session Label, Silhouette and TBH. Henkel also sells
adhesive technologies in Australia.
The target, Olaplex, is a Delaware-registered company listed on the
Nasdaq Global Select Market and headquartered in New York. In
Australia, Olaplex primarily supplies premium hair products to online
retailers, specialty retailers and hair professionals.

determination_table_content.item:
Overlap and relationship
between the parties

Henkel and Olaplex overlap in the supply of hair care products to
professional and retail customers.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition
and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition
assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an
acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in
Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have
regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the
interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines
and interim merger process guidelines.
In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered
the information and documents that were submitted with the
notification form, information from third parties, and publicly
available information.
The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into
effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the
effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In
reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC
makes the following findings.
• The market share aggregation resulting from the Acquisition is
estimated to be low.
• The merged firm would likely continue to face competition from
multiple alternative suppliers of hair care products to both
professional and retail customers in Australia.

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by
the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are
dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the
Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to
the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar
days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s
Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any
application for review, please contact the Australian Competition
Tribunal.

deals_cleared.merger_name: Bingo Industries - JLW Services
deals_cleared.merger_description: BINGO Acquisitions Pty Ltd, trading as BINGO Industries (**BINGO**), proposes to acquire the assets and the business of JLW Services Pty Ltd (**JLW**) (the **Acquisition**).

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
BINGO Acquisitions Pty Ltd, trading as BINGO Industries (Bingo
Industries), applied for a notification waiver in respect of its
proposed acquisition of the assets of JLW Services Pty Ltd (JLW
Services), as described in the transaction documents provided as
part of the application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.
20 June 2026

The acquirer, Bingo Industries is a recycling and waste management
company with operations in New South Wales, Victoria and
Queensland. Bingo Industries provides collection, processing,
recycling and disposal services for building and demolition waste as
well as for commercial and industrial waste.
The target, JLW Services, is based in Cootamundra, New South
Wales, and provides collection, processing and recycling services for
end-of-life tyres and mattresses, as well as onsite shredding and
mine site clean-up services, servicing mainly regional locations in
New South Wales, Queensland, Victoria and South Australia.
Both parties are active in the supply of collection, processing and
recycling services and alternative fuel sources in New South Wales,
Victoria and Queensland.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information provided in the application, the ACCC
considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material
lessening of competition. In particular:
a. there is a small horizontal overlap between Bingo Industries
and JLW Services in the supply of collection, processing and
recycling services in specialised waste streams, in particular
tyres and mattresses in New South Wales, Victoria and
Queensland
b. there is a small horizontal overlap between Bingo Industries
and JLW Services in the supply of alternative fuel sources to
customers in New South Wales, Victoria and Queensland
c. there are alternative suppliers of collection, processing and
recycling services in specialised waste streams, in particular
tyres and mattresses in New South Wales, Victoria and
Queensland
d. there are alternative suppliers alternative fuel sources in
New South Wales, Victoria and Queensland.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

acquirers.name: AIPCF VIII Global Corp Holding L.P.

deals_cleared.merger_description: Certain affiliates of AIP, LLC. (together, **AIP**) propose to acquire all of the outstanding equity interests in the Warehouse and Workflow Solutions (**WWS**) business of Honeywell International, Inc. (**Honeywell**) (the **Acquisition**).

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Certain affiliates of AIP, LLC. (together, AIP) propose to acquire all of
the outstanding equity interests in the Warehouse and Workflow
Solutions (WWS) business of Honeywell International, Inc.
(Honeywell), as described in the transaction documents provided as
part of the application (the Acquisition).
Honeywell’s WWS business is comprised of Ibiza Holdings, Inc and
Honeywell Intelligrated GmbH and their respective subsidiaries
(together, Honeywell WWS).
AIPCF VIII Global Corp Holding L.P. applied for a notification waiver
in respect of the Acquisition.

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.
20 June 2026

The acquirer, AIP, is an operationally oriented middle-market private
equity firm based in New York, US, with a focus on the acquisition
and improvement of industrial businesses. As part of the Acquisition,
AIP has established wholly owned special-purpose vehicles which
will be the direct acquirers of the equity in Honeywell WWS.
The target, Honeywell WWS, is headquartered in Mason, Ohio (U.S)
and is a provider of material handling and warehouse automation
solutions. Honeywell WWS is currently owned by Honeywell, which is
a US-based publicly traded multinational conglomerate
headquartered in Charlotte, North Carolina, United States of America.
Honeywell primarily operates in four business areas: (i) Aerospace
Technologies, (ii) Building Automation, (iii) Energy and Sustainability
Solutions, and (iv) Industrial Automation.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
certain publicly available information, and had regard to the factors
in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition and Consumer Act 2010
(Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information currently before it, the ACCC considers that
the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material lessening of
competition. In particular:
a. there is no overlap between AIP, its connected entities and
Honeywell WWS in the supply of material handling and
automated warehouse solutions in Australia
b. there are alternative suppliers of material handling and
automated warehouse solutions in Australia
c. Honeywell WWS has a small presence in Australia.
In these circumstances, the ACCC does not consider it necessary to
reach a concluded view on the likelihood of the notification
thresholds being met.
Given that material competition concerns are unlikely to arise, the
ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
River Capital Pty Ltd (River Capital) applied for a notification waiver
in respect of its proposed acquisition, through a special purpose
vehicle (RC Bite Bid Co Pty Ltd), of 100% of the issued shares in
YOMG OpCo (which will own the trading business operating under
the “YOMG” brand), the Leaseholder Companies (which hold the
leases for each YOMG location), and YOMG IP Co (which holds the
YOMG Group's intellectual property), as described in the transaction
documents provided as part of the application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.
22 June 2026

The acquirer, River Capital, is an Australian-incorporated private
investment firm headquartered in Melbourne, Australia. River Capital
manages a number of funds with varying strategies and investments
in various portfolio companies.
The Cheesecake Shop, a River Capital portfolio company, is a retailer
of cakes and desserts. It sells whole cakes and slices baked fresh
on-site for take-home consumption, ordered in-store or online, as
well as “dessert cups”. The Cheesecake Shop has approximately 255
stores across Australia and New Zealand.
The Yo My Company Pty Ltd and its associated entities operate food
and beverage outlets in Australia under the “YOMG” brand. YOMG is
a quick-service restaurant business supplying burgers, fries, wings,
frozen yoghurt and related food and beverage products. YOMG
currently operates 15 stores across Victoria and Queensland.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information provided in the application, the ACCC
considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material
lessening of competition. In particular:
a. the parties’ combined share in the retail supply of dessert
products in Victoria, Queensland and Australia is estimated
to be low and the increase in share arising from the
Acquisition would be small
b. there are alternative retail suppliers of dessert products near
YOMG stores in Victoria and Queensland.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

deals_cleared.merger_name: Hexagon - Waygate Technologies

targets.name: BAKER HUGHES DIGITAL SOLUTIONS AUSTRALIA PTY LTD

deals_cleared.merger_description: Hexagon AB (**Hexagon**), through its subsidiary, Hexagon Manufacturing Intelligence LLC, is proposing to acquire the Waygate business, including certain assets of Baker Hughes Digital Solutions Australia Pty Ltd (together, **Waygate**).

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Hexagon AB (Hexagon), through its subsidiary Hexagon
Manufacturing Intelligence LLC, applied for a notification waiver in
respect of its proposed acquisition of the Waygate business,
including certain assets of Baker Hughes Digital Solutions Australia
Pty Ltd (together, Waygate), as described in the transaction
documents provided as part of the application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.
22 June 2026

The acquirer, Hexagon, is a multinational industrial technology
company headquartered in Stockholm. Together with its
subsidiaries, Hexagon supplies a range of measuring technology and
geospatial tools and software. In Australia, Hexagon is involved in
the provision of non-destructive testing (NDT) solutions, including
through the supply of its Volume Graphics software. This software is
used for industrial computed tomography-based non-destructive
evaluation, to enable the detection of internal defects, dimensional
measurement, and material analysis without damaging components.
The target, Waygate, is currently indirectly wholly owned and
controlled by Baker Hughes Company and its subsidiary, Baker
Hughes Holdings LLC.
Waygate is an industrial technology company specialising in NDT
and inspection solutions used to assess the integrity, quality, and
performance of materials, components, and industrial assets. Its
core business involves the development and supply of integrated
hardware, software, and services that enable customers to detect
defects, verify quality, and monitor the condition of critical assets
through their lifecycle. Waygate’s range of NDT technologies are
used to inspect both the internal and external characteristics of
components, supporting applications such as dimensional
measurement, defect detection, and structural analysis.
The parties are both global providers of NDT solutions.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
certain publicly available information, and had regard to the factors
in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition and Consumer Act 2010
(Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information currently before it, the ACCC considers that
the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material lessening of
competition. In particular:
a. there is limited overlap between the parties in the supply
of NDT solutions and software in Australia, as their
respective offerings primarily serve different use cases
b. there are alternative providers of the types of NDT
solutions and software offered by the parties available in
Australia
c. there appears to be a low risk of foreclosure or other
concerning vertical or conglomerate effects resulting
from exclusionary conduct, bundling or tying of products
post-acquisition.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

deals_cleared.merger_description: Action Supermarkets Pty Ltd, a subsidiary of Metcash Limited (together, **Metcash**), proposes to acquire all of the business and assets of ZH Supermarket Pty Ltd, an independent supermarket trading as Foodland Flagstaff Hill in South Australia (**Foodland Flagstaff Hill**).

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Action Supermarkets Pty Ltd, a subsidiary of Metcash Limited
(ASX:MTS) (Metcash), applied for a notification waiver in respect of
its proposed acquisition of the business and assets of ZH
Supermarket Pty Ltd (Foodland Flagstaff Hill), as described in the
transaction documents provided as part of the application (the
Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.
22 June 2026

The acquirer, Metcash, is an ASX-listed wholesaler and distributor of
food, liquor and hardware products. Metcash’s supermarkets
division supplies packaged groceries and fresh produce to a network
of around 2,500 independently owned supermarket stores Australia-
wide, including to its banner groups IGA and Foodland. Metcash
provides its banner groups with brand licensing, marketing and
promotional support. Metcash also services non-bannered stores
and third-party groups such as FoodWorks.
In addition to its wholesale operations, Metcash currently operates 3
retail supermarkets in Australia – an IGA store in Turramurra NSW,
and 2 Foodland stores in Goolwa and Naracoorte in South Australia.
Metcash’s supermarkets in South Australia are located
approximately 70km and 338km away from Foodland Flagstaff Hill,
respectively.
The target, Foodland Flagstaff Hill, is an independent supermarket
operating under the Foodland banner and is a wholesale customer of
Metcash. It is located in the Flagstaff Hill Shopping Centre Precinct,
approximately 18 kilometres south of the Adelaide central business
district.
There is a vertical relationship between the parties as Metcash
supplies wholesale grocery products to Foodland Flagstaff Hill.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information currently before it, the ACCC considers that
the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material lessening of
competition. In particular:
a. there is no horizontal overlap between Metcash and
Foodland Flagstaff Hill in the retail supply of groceries in the
Flagstaff Hill and surrounding areas
b. there are alternative retail supermarkets in Flagstaff Hill and
surrounding areas
c. there is a low risk of foreclosure or other concerning vertical
effects resulting from exclusionary conduct post-acquisition.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

Ltd (**APS**), pursuant to a share purchase agreement (the **Acquisition**). APS is currently wholly owned by Handa Manufacturing Pty Ltd as trustee for the Handa Manufacturing Trust.

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
SHAPE Australia Corporation Limited (SHAPE) applied for a
notification waiver in respect of its proposed acquisition of 100% of
the issued share capital in Australian Professional Shopfitters Pty.
Ltd (APS), as described in the transaction documents provided as
part of the application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.
22 June 2026

The acquirer, SHAPE, is an Australian publicly listed company (ASX:
SHA). SHAPE provides a diverse range of construction services and
products, including facilities maintenance, fitouts, and new builds, to
customers in the office, transport, hotel, health, education, retail
including retail fuel, government, aged care, community, data
centres, defence, entertainment and recreation, industrial, and
transport sectors in Australia.
The target, APS, provides commercial fitout services and products,
including shopfitting, manufacturing, project management and
transport services, to customers in the retail sector in Australia. APS
also has a warehouse in Melbourne supplying custom joinery for
fitouts, almost all of which is supplied in-house for its own fitout
projects.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
certain publicly available information, and had regard to the factors
in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition and Consumer Act 2010
(Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information currently before it, the ACCC considers that
the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material lessening of
competition. In particular:
a. there is a small horizontal overlap between SHAPE Australia
and APS in the supply of commercial fitout services in the
retail sector, and the increase in SHAPE’s share of supply of
commercial fitout services to non-residential customers is
estimated to be small
b. there are alternative suppliers supplying commercial fitout
services and joinery to customers, including for the retail
sector, at both national and local level
c. there is a low risk of foreclosure or other concerning vertical
or conglomerate effects resulting from exclusionary
conduct, bundling or tying of services post-acquisition.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

deals_cleared.merger_name: Imagine Education – childcare centres ownership restructure

acquirers.name: POLAR 993 PTY LTD as trustee of Imagine Education Investments Trust

deals_cleared.merger_description: The parties propose to undertake an ownership restructure between the existing part-owners of 35 childcare centres that form part of the Imagine Education group, have a common management team and operate under the Imagine Childcare, Children First and Grow Early Education brands (the **Acquisition**).

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Woodward pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Waterworks Education Group Pty Ltd (WEG) applied for a notification
waiver in respect of the proposed ownership restructure between the
existing part-owners of 35 childcare centres that form part of the
Imagine Education group and operate through 34 unit trusts (Sub-
Trusts), as described in the transaction documents provided as part
of the application (the Acquisition).
The Acquisition has two stages.
1. In the first stage, 4 Sub-Trusts (AH Sub-Trusts) propose to
acquire all of the units they do not already hold in 4 unit
trusts through which 4 of the childcare centres are operated
(AH Sub-Sub-Trusts). In exchange, the AH Sub-Trusts
propose to issue the current holders of those units with new
units in the AH Sub-Trusts. As a result, Imagine Education
Investments Trust (Head Trust) would increase its
unitholding in 2 AH Sub-Trusts.
2. In the second stage, Head Trust proposes to acquire all of
the units that it does not already hold in each of the Sub-
Trusts from the current holders of those units (Vendors), in
exchange for Head Trustee issuing the Vendors with new
units in Head Trust. As a result, WEG’s interest in Head Trust
would be diluted but it would acquire an interest in the units
in the Sub-Trusts to be acquired by Head Trust.

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.
22 June 2026

The restructure relates to childcare centres that form part of the
Imagine Education group. These centres provide centre based child
care and early childhood education services (long day care) for
children aged approximately 0–6 years, and ancillary services
associated with the operation of childcare centres (for example,
meals and incidental educational programmes).
Currently, Head Trust holds a proportion of the units in most of the
Sub-Trusts and all of the units in Head Trust are owned by WEG. The
current trustee of Head Trust is Imagine Education Investments Pty
Ltd. Prior to the Acquisition, Polar 993 Limited (Head Trustee) will be
appointed as the new trustee of Head Trust.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information provided in the application, the ACCC
considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material
lessening of competition. In particular, there is no horizontal
consolidation, removal of a competitor, or change to Imagine
Education group’s competitive position as a result of the Acquisition.
In these circumstances, the ACCC does not consider it necessary to
reach a concluded view on the likelihood of the notification
thresholds being met.
Given that material competition concerns are unlikely to arise, the
ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.
The ACCC also considered the potential application of section
51ABD of the Act. However, the applicant submits that the
Acquisition does not fall within that provision and, having regard to
the particular circumstances, including the consequences of non-
notification in reliance on the exemption if it was found not to apply,
the ACCC considered it appropriate to make a determination on the
basis that the acquisition provisions in the Act apply to the
Acquisition.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

deals_cleared.merger_description: Mercer Investments (Australia) Limited (**MIAL**) as trustee for AP#2 Trust proposes to acquire a 49.9% interest in the Stockland Residential Rental Partnership (**SRRP**) portfolio from One MEA Holdings Pte. Ltd (**One MEA**).

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Mercer Investments (Australia) Limited (MIAL), as trustee for AP#2
Trust, applied for a notification waiver in respect of its proposed
acquisition of a 49.9% interest in the Stockland Residential Rental
Partnership (SRRP) portfolio from One MEA Holdings Pte. Ltd (One
MEA), as described in the transaction documents provided as part of
the application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.
23 June 2026

The acquirer, MIAL, is a wholly owned subsidiary of Mercer
(Australia) Pty Ltd (Mercer Australia), which is part of the Mercer
group of companies (Mercer). Mercer is a member of the Marsh &
McLennan Companies, a group of professional services companies
offering advice and solutions in the areas of risk, strategy, and
human capital.
MIAL is the trustee of AP#2 Trust, an investment vehicle established
and managed by MIAL for the purposes of investing in real estate
assets in Australia on behalf of the Mercer Super Trust, a registered
and qualifying superannuation fund.
The target, SRRP, is focused on the development and long-term
ownership of land lease communities. As at the proposed
completion date of the Acquisition, the SRRP portfolio will be
comprised of 5 stabilised communities and one community partly
complete and partly under development, with the total portfolio
including approximately 2,000 homes across Queensland and
Victoria.
SRRP was established by Stockland and Mitsubishi Estate Asia
(MEA) in 2022. MEA is the international real estate development and
investment arm of the Japanese property firm, Mitsubishi Estate Co.
Ltd (MEC). One MEA is the investment vehicle owned by MEC and
managed by MEA. Headquartered in Singapore since 2008, MEA
oversees MEC’s regional operations in countries including Vietnam,
Indonesia, Thailand, and Australia.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application, and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information currently before it, the ACCC considers
that the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material lessening
of competition. In particular:
a. the Acquisition will not result in any substantive change in
the control or day-to-day operation of the SRRP Portfolio
b. there is limited geographic overlap between the land lease
community developments of the parties’ connected entities.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

deals_cleared.merger_description: Precision Poultry Pty Ltd (**Precision Poultry**) proposes to acquire the broiler chicken farming operations of VOAG Pty Ltd and its related entities (**VOAG**) through coordinated asset and land acquisitions (the **Acquisition**).

events.title: Questionnaire - Precision Poultry - VOAG chicken farming operations
events.display_title: Questionnaire - Precision Poultry - VOAG chicken farming operations
events.url_gh: /mergers/MN-35019/Precision Poultry - VOAG - Questionnaire_0.pdf

events.title: Precision Poultry - VOAG chicken farming operations - Phase 1 determination
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Precision Poultry Pty Ltd (Precision Poultry)’s proposed acquisition
of the broiler chicken farming operations of VOAG Pty Ltd and its
related entities (together, VOAG) (the Acquisition).
Pursuant to the Acquisition, Precision Poultry proposes to acquire:
• broiler chicken farming business assets from VOAG Pty Ltd, and
• the freehold land on which the farming assets are operated from
subsidiaries of VOAG 6 Pty Ltd, the head landholder entity of the
vendor, being VOAG 1 Pty Ltd, VOAG 2 Pty Ltd, VOAG 3 Pty Ltd,
VOAG 4 Pty Ltd and VOAG 5 Pty Ltd.

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The acquirer, Precision Poultry, has been established as an
investment vehicle by private equity firm Roc Partners to provide
agribusiness investment returns to Roc Partners-controlled or
managed entities. Precision Poultry’s broiler chicken farming
operations are located in Victoria.
The target is the broiler chicken farm operations of VOAG, located in
Tabbita, approximately 33 kms north-west of Griffith in New South
Wales. It supplies broiler chicken growing services to Baiada
Poultry’s chicken meat processing facility near Griffith.

Precision Poultry and VOAG (the Parties) overlap in the supply of
broiler chicken growing services in Australia.

Broilers are chickens grown for the purpose of chicken meat
consumption. Broiler chicken growing services involve rearing day-
old chicks into processing-ready broiler chickens under long-term
grower contracts.
When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition
and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition
assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an
acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in
Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have
regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the
interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines
and interim merger process guidelines.
In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered
the information and documents that were submitted with the
notification form, information from third partes and publicly available
information, including the ACCC’s Perishable Goods Inquiry report
2020.
The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into
effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the
effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In
reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC
found that Precision Poultry and VOAG do not compete closely in the
supply of broiler chicken growing services due to a lack of regional
overlap in the parties’ operations.

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by
the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are
dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the
Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to
the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar
days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s
Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any
application for review, please contact the Australian Competition
Tribunal.

events.title: PT Asian Bulk Logistics - Engage Marine - Phase 1 determination
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Philip Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
PT Asian Bulk Logistics, through its Australian subsidiary, Global
Engage Marine Pty Ltd (ABL) proposes to acquire Engage Marine
TopCo Pty Ltd (Engage Marine) (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The acquirer, ABL operates three subsidiaries in Australia:
▪ Transhipment Services Australia, which provides bulk cargo
transhipment services utilising floating barges to transfer
materials, such as iron ore, directly between smaller ships or
shallow terminals and larger ocean-going vessels in
deepwater berths.
▪ One Rail Australia, a rail freight operator operating in NSW
and central Queensland, specialising in transporting bulk
commodities such as coal and minerals.
▪ SCF Containers, an Australian shipping container supplier
offering a wide range of products for hire or sale, including
shipping containers, site sheds, dangerous goods
containers, tank and refrigerated containers, intermodal
containers, trailers, and remote camps.
The target, Engage Marine, provides terminal and harbour towage
(towage services) including the berthing and sailing of ocean-going
vessels at ports across Australia. Engage Marine also provides ad-
hoc towage emergency response solutions in salvage situations.
Transhipment in this context refers to a logistics service concerned
with getting a mined commodity from land to an ocean-going vessel.

determination_table_content.item:
Overlap and relationship
between the parties
determination_table_content.details:
The merger parties have a vertical relationship with Engage Marine’s
towage services being an input into the supply of transhipment
services in some circumstances.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition
and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition
assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an
acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in
Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have
regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the
interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines
and interim merger process guidelines.
In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered
the information and documents that were submitted with the
notification form, information from third partes and publicly available
information.
The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into
effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the
effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In
reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC
makes the following findings.
• The merger parties are not close competitors in the supply of
transhipment or towage services.
• A vertical relationship between towage services and
transhipment services may arise at a port. However, the merged
firm is unlikely to have the ability and incentive to foreclose rival
transhipment service providers post-acquisition.
o This is because there are alternative options to Engage
Marine available to transhipment providers at those ports
where there is a current demand for transhipment services.
Those options include alternative towage service providers
currently operating at the port or the ability of some
transhipment customers to sponsor a new provider of
towage services to deliver the services they require at that
port.

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by
the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are
dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the
Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to
the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar
days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s
Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any
application for review, please contact the Australian Competition
Tribunal.

deals_cleared.merger_name: B.M.D. Holdings – Bajara Plant Hire and Stirling Contracting
deals_cleared.merger_description: B.M.D. Holdings Pty. Limited the ultimate holding company of ‘BMD Group’ (the **Acquirer**) proposes to acquire a minority equity position in each of Bajara Plant Hire Pty Ltd (**Bajara**) and Stirling Contracting Pty Ltd (a newly incorporated entity that will acquire the assets of the earthworks business currently owned by Stirling Earthworks Pty Ltd, Stirling) (together, the **Target**) by way of Share Purchase Agreement (**Proposed Acquisition**).

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
B.M.D. Holdings Pty. Limited the ultimate holding company of BMD
Group (the Acquirer) applied for a notification waiver in respect to its
proposed acquisition of a minority equity position in each of Bajara
Plant Hire Pty Ltd (Bajara) and Stirling Contracting Pty Ltd (a newly
incorporated entity that will acquire the assets of the earthworks
business currently owned by Stirling Earthworks Pty Ltd, Stirling)
(together, the Target) by way of Share Purchase Agreement
(Proposed Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.
24 June 2026

The Acquirer, BMD Group operates across civil construction,
engineering design, residential and industrial land development,
project management consultancy, landscape construction and
maintenance, and building construction. Headquartered in Brisbane,
it operates nationwide across Australia.
Relevantly, ‘BMD Constructions’ is BMD Group’s construction arm. It
delivers complex infrastructure projects across sectors including
defence, water and wastewater, transport, ports, aviation and
renewable energy; through the provision of civil and building works
for land development and associated infrastructure. BMD self-
supplies both plant and equipment, and earthworks services but
does not offer these services to third parties.
The Target operates two businesses in the construction industry:
1. Bajara, which supplies civil construction and mining-specific
plant and equipment for both wet and dry hire; and
2. Stirling, which supplies earthworks services.
Also headquartered in Brisbane, the Target delivers small-to-
medium-sized infrastructure and resources projects, predominantly
in Queensland, and including to BMD.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information provided in the application, the ACCC
considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material
lessening of competition. In particular:
a. there is limited horizontal overlap between the parties in the
supply of plant and equipment hire for civil construction and
mining, or earthworks services in Queensland
b. there are alternative suppliers of plant and equipment hire
for civil construction and mining, and earthworks services in
Queensland
c. there is a low risk of foreclosure or other concerning vertical
effects resulting from exclusionary conduct post-acquisition.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

deals_cleared.merger_description: Gunvor Group Ltd, or a wholly-owned Australian subsidiary nominated by Gunvor Group Ltd, proposes to acquire by way of a share purchase agreement 100% of the issued share capital of ZEN Energy Retail Holdings Pty Ltd (the **Target**) from ZEN Energy Retail Pty Ltd (**ZER**) (the **Acquisition**).

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Gunvor Group Ltd applied for a notification waiver in respect of its
(or a wholly-owned Australian subsidiary nominated by Gunvor
Group Ltd) proposed acquisition of 100% of the issued share capital
of ZEN Energy Retail Holdings Pty Ltd (the Target) from ZEN Energy
Retail Pty Ltd (ZER), as described in the transaction documents
provided as part of the application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.
24 June 2026

The acquirer, Gunvor Group Ltd, is the main holding company of the
Gunvor Group (Gunvor), a global commodity trading house that is
registered in Cyprus and has an energy trading business. While
Gunvor does not currently operate a physical business in Australia, it
is a supplier of fuel to Australian fuel importers and a participant in
the wholesale electricity derivative contracts market within the
National Electricity Market (NEM). Gunvor is not involved in the
wholesale generation, or the retail supply, of electricity in the NEM or
elsewhere in Australia, and it does not own or control any operating
energy generation assets or infrastructure.
ZER (and its affiliates, together the ZEN Energy Group) is an
Australian renewable energy company which supplies electricity to
large use customers across the NEM. The ZEN Energy Group
operates two business units: a retail customer business with an
associated hedging portfolio, and an asset development program. Its
hedging portfolio primarily comprises long-term wholesale derivative
contracts, including power purchase agreements (PPAs) with
renewable generators, and tolling leases over operating battery
energy storage systems (BESS).
The Target, which has been newly incorporated for the purpose of
the Acquisition, will hold the assets relating to the ZEN Energy
Group’s retail business, including all of ZER's retail electricity supply
customer contracts and a hedging portfolio consisting of three PPAs
(relating to the Tailem Bend 2 Hybrid Project, New England Solar
Farm and Moree Solar Farm) and a virtual toll, energy storage
services agreement and other synthetic battery offtake agreement in
respect of the Templers BESS facility, together with employees,
intellectual property, licences, and IT systems. Prior to completion,
ZER will transfer the assets within the sale perimeter to the Target.
The Acquisition is limited to the ZEN Energy retail business and three
PPAs and the tolling agreement within its hedging portfolio, and
does not include the asset development business unit owned by Zen
Energy Pty Ltd or the remainder of the hedging portfolio.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information currently before it, the ACCC considers
that the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material lessening
of competition. In particular:
a. there is no horizontal overlap between Gunvor and the
Target in the retail supply of electricity to large use
customers in the NEM
b. post-Acquisition Gunvor’s share in the acquisition of
wholesale derivative contracts for renewable energy and
battery storage assets in the NEM and in Queensland, NSW,
Victoria, South Australia and Tasmania is estimated to be
low
c. there are numerous alternative retail suppliers of electricity
to large use customers, and alternative acquirers of
wholesale derivative contracts for renewable energy and
battery storage assets in the NEM and across Australia
d. there appears to be a low risk of foreclosure or other
concerning vertical effects resulting from exclusionary
conduct post-acquisition.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

deals_cleared.merger_name: Aula Energy – Additional interest in the Boulder Creek Wind Farm
deals_cleared.merger_description: This application for waiver relates to the Boulder Creek Wind Farm Project (**BCWF Project**) and a proposal by Aula Energy Holdings Pty Ltd as trustee for the Aula Energy Asset Hold Trust (**Aula Energy**) to acquire the remaining 50% of the BCWF Project that it does not already own (the **Acquisition**).

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Aula Energy Holdings Pty Ltd as trustee for the Aula Energy Asset
Hold Trust and its wholly owned indirect subsidiary, BCWF 2
Holdings Pty Ltd, (together, Aula Energy) applied for a notification
waiver in respect of Aula Energy’s proposed acquisition of the
remaining 50% interest in the Boulder Creek Wind Farm Project that
it does not already own, as described in the transaction documents
provided as part of the application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.
24 June 2026

The acquirer, Aula Energy, is a renewable energy developer
established in 2023 which is wholly owned by funds managed by
Macquarie Asset Management, an operating group of Macquarie
Group Limited. Aula Energy owns five operational solar generation
assets and has a number of other renewable energy projects in
development.
The Boulder Creek Wind Farm Project (BCWF Project) involves the
development, construction, ownership and operation of a wind farm
at Boulder Creek, located approximately 40 km southwest of
Rockhampton and 5 km west of Mt Morgan in central Queensland.
The BCWF Project is currently under construction with the first stage
of completion anticipated to occur by November 2027. On
completion, the BCWF Project will supply electricity on a wholesale
basis to the National Electricity Market (NEM).
Aula Energy has a pre-existing 50% interest in the BCWF Project.
Under the Acquisition, Aula Energy will acquire the remaining 50%
interest in the BCWF Project which is currently held by CS Energy,
which is a Queensland-based Government‑owned energy company.
The Acquisition will increase Aula Energy’s ownership of electricity
generation assets in the NEM.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information currently before it, the ACCC considers that
the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material lessening of
competition. In particular:
a. post-Acquisition, Aula Energy’s share in the supply of
wholesale electricity generation in the NEM, including in
Queensland, is estimated to be low and the increase in share
of supply arising from the Acquisition would be small
b. there are alternative suppliers of wholesale electricity
generation in the NEM, including in Queensland.
In these circumstances, the ACCC does not consider it necessary to
reach a concluded view on the likelihood of the notification
thresholds being met.
Given that material competition concerns are unlikely to arise, the
ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

determination_table_content.details:
Tasmea Limited (Tasmea) applied for a notification waiver in respect
of its proposed acquisition of 100% of the share capital in Maxim

documents provided as part of the application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.

The acquirer, Tasmea, is an Australian diversified specialist
trade‑skill services group. It owns and operates 26 specialist
subsidiaries providing maintenance, shutdown, breakdown and
brownfield upgrade services to fixed plant for essential asset
owners, primarily in the resources, energy and utilities sectors.
Tasmea’s electrical division or segment delivers industrial electrical,
instrumentation and electrification services, principally in Western
Australia, as well as Queensland, South Australia and the Northern
Territory.
The target, Maxim, headquartered in Alphington Victoria and
operates through Maxim Electrical Services Pty Ltd and Maxim
Infrastructure Pty Ltd. Maxim delivers project and construction
based electrical works for data centre owners, Tier 1 builders and
head contractors, including major Victorian government
infrastructure programs. Maxim’s services are principally acquired
by data centres, transport infrastructure operators and other
commercial sector projects.
The parties both supply electrical services and maintenance across

competition. In particular:
a. there is limited horizontal overlap between Tasmea and

across Australia, as their respective offering service
different use cases and geographic regions
b. there are alternative suppliers of the types of electrical
services and maintenance offered by the parties in Australia.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the

While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely

determination_table_content.details:
Australia and New Zealand Banking Group Limited (ANZ) proposes
to acquire Worldline SA (WL)’s interest in Worldline Australia Pty Ltd,
trading as ANZ Worldline Payment Solutions (ANZWL), under a
share sale and purchase agreement (the Acquisition). Following the
Acquisition, ANZ Group will wholly own ANZWL.

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The acquirer, ANZ, is an entity within a group of companies
ultimately held by ANZ Group Holdings Limited (ANZ Group). ANZ
Group is an ASX-listed financial services company. ANZ provides a
range of banking products and services to retail and business
customers in Australia and New Zealand, and to institutional
customers globally.
The target, ANZWL, provides merchant acquiring and related
payment services to business customers in Australia. ANZWL is
currently a joint venture between ANZ and WL, with ANZ Group
holding 49% and WL holding 51% of the share capital of ANZWL.
WL is a global merchant acquiring business headquartered in France
and Europe’s largest merchant acquiring services provider.

determination_table_content.item:
Overlap and relationship
between the parties
determination_table_content.details:
ANZ has an ongoing commercial relationship with ANZWL, including
referral arrangements for business customers requiring merchant
acquiring services.
Suncorp, part of the ANZ Group, separately offers merchant
acquiring and related services to business customers in Australia.
The Parties overlap in the supply of business payment solutions,
including merchant acquiring, payment acceptance and value-added
services, to business customers in Australia.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition
and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition
assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an
acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in
Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have
regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the
interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines
and interim merger process guidelines.
In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered
the information and documents that were submitted with the
notification form and publicly available information.
The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into
effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the
effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In
reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC
makes the following findings.
• The merged entity’s market share is estimated to be low, and
ANZ would likely continue to be constrained by alternative
suppliers of business payment solutions in Australia.

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by
the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are
dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the
Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to
the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar
days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s
Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any
application for review, please contact the Australian Competition
Tribunal.

events.title: Impression Dental Group - Coastal Dental Care - Phase 1 determination
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
General Dental Holdings Pty Ltd (trading as Impression Dental
Group) (IDG) proposes to acquire 100% of the shares in Coastal
Dental Care Australia Pty Ltd (CDC), through a share sale deed.

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

CDC is owned by Dr Franck Page and operates 15 dental practices
across the Redland Bay and Gold Coast areas in Queensland and in
Northern NSW under the Coastal Dental Care, Dental Implants &
Aesthetics, and Gold Coast Holistic Dental Care names. CDC also
has a dental laboratory (The Lab), a denture clinic (Gold Coast
Dentures by Design) and a dental supplies organisation (Dental
Plus).
IDG is an Australian-based dental support organisation that provides
centralised services, including recruitment, payroll, marketing, IT,
finance, and operation, to dental practitioners. IDG currently owns
and supports 23 independently branded dental practices in Australia
which provide dental services to the public, but IDG does not have
any practices in the Redland Bay and Gold Coast areas or Northern
NSW.
IDG is controlled by Genesis Capital, an Australian-based private
equity firm that invests in businesses operating in the healthcare
sector.
Genesis Capital’s investments also include Pacific Smiles Group
(PSG) which sets up new dental centres from which dentists run
their practice and provides operational support. PSG has over 120
dental centres across Australia, including 1 in the Redland Bay area,
5 on the Gold Coast, and 1 in Tweed. PSG’s dental centres are
typically located inside shopping centres and operate under the
Pacific Smiles Dental and nib Dental Care brands on the East Coast.

determination_table_content.item:
Overlap and relationship
between the parties
determination_table_content.details:
The parties overlap in the supply of dentistry services.
CDC also supplies products used in the supply of dentistry services,
including dentures, mouthguards and retainers, crown and bridge
laboratory services and intraoral cameras.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition
and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition
assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an
acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in
Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have
regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the
interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines
and interim merger process guidelines.
In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered
the information and documents that were submitted with the
notification form, information from third parties and publicly
available information.
The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into
effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the
effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In
reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC
makes the following findings.
• The aggregation in the supply of dentistry services in the local
areas surrounding CDC’s clinics arising from the Acquisition,
including any specialist dentistry services, is not significant.
When combined with previous acquisitions by IDG in the last
three years, the Acquisition is not likely to affect competition in
local areas or any broader market.
• IDG will continue to face competition post Acquisition from a
number of alternative dental clinics in the local areas
surrounding CDC’s clinics.
• Post acquisition, IDG would be unlikely to have the ability and
incentive to foreclose competitors’ access to inputs given the
presence of multiple alternative suppliers of dentures,
mouthguards and retainers, crown and bridge laboratory
services, and intraoral cameras.

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by
the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are
dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the
Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to
the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar
days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s
Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any
application for review, please contact the Australian Competition
Tribunal.

determination_table_content.details:

deals_cleared.merger_name: ANZ, CBA, NAB and Westpac – shares in joint venture and interests in Joint Cash Pool

acquirers.name: AUSTRALIA AND NEW ZEALAND BANKING GROUP LIMITED
NATIONAL AUSTRALIA INVESTMENT CAPITAL PTY LTD

deals_cleared.merger_description: ANZ, CBA, NAB and Westpac (together, the **Major Banks**) propose to enter into a joint venture for the establishment of a new joint single cash pool (**Joint Cash Pool**) between the Major Banks with respect to wholesale and ‘commercial cash’ distribution (**Proposed JV**).

events.title: ANZ, CBA, NAB and Westpac - shares in  joint venture and interests in Joint Cash Pool - Phase 1 determination
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Australia and New Zealand Banking Group Limited (ANZ),
Commonwealth Bank of Australia (CBA), National Australia Bank
Limited and National Australia Investment Capital Pty Ltd (NAB), and
Westpac Banking Corporation (Westpac) (together, the Major Banks)
propose to enter into a joint venture for the establishment of a new
joint single cash pool (Joint Cash Pool) between the Major Banks
with respect to wholesale and ‘commercial cash’ distribution (the
Proposed JV).
As part of the Proposed JV, each Major Bank will acquire:
• a 25% shareholding in an incorporated operating entity (JVCo),
and
• a 25% interest in the Joint Cash Pool
(together, the Acquisitions).
The Acquisitions represent a narrow part of the arrangements in
relation to the establishment of JVCo and the Joint Cash Pool. The
remaining conduct relating to the establishment and operation of
JVCo and the Joint Cash Pool is the subject of a separate
application for authorisation (AA1000729) under section 88 of
the Competition and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act) (the
Authorisation Application).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Act that the Acquisitions
may be put into effect.

Each Major Bank is a party to the Proposed JV. Currently, the Major
Banks purchase banknotes from the RBA and coins from the Mint
directly and each Major Bank has its own cash reserves, which are
managed as four separate ‘virtual’ cash pools. Each Major Bank
uses their own cash pool to support the supply of cash to their own
branches and ATMs, and directly to business customers in some
instances.
Westpac is currently the sole supplier of commercial cash to
distributors other than the Major Banks, including cash-in-transit
providers (Commercial Cash Distributors). Customers of
Commercial Cash Distributors include smaller banks, major retailers
and small businesses. Westpac has decided to cease its current
arrangements as the sole supplier of commercial cash.
Once the Proposed JV is established, and subject to the outcome of
the separate Authorisation Application, the Proposed JV would:
• hold the cash in the Joint Cash Pool as bailee for the Major
Banks as joint bailors
• supply cash from the Joint Cash Pool to each of the Major
Banks (in their capacity as customers of JVCo), in order to
meet their cash requirements, including for their own branches
and ATMs, and
• become the sole supplier of commercial cash to in-scope
Commercial Cash Distributors from the Joint Cash Pool.

determination_table_content.item:
Overlap and relationship
between the parties
determination_table_content.details:
Relevantly, the Major Banks currently overlap in the acquisition,
supply and distribution of banknotes and coins in Australia.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition
and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition
assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an
acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in
Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Act. In doing so,
the ACCC must have regard to the object of the Act and all relevant
matters, including the interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines
and interim merger process guidelines.
In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered
the information and documents that were submitted with the
notification form and the Authorisation Application, as well as
information provided by third parties regarding the Acquisitions and
interim request for authorisation.
The ACCC has determined that the Acquisitions may be put into
effect as it considers that the Acquisitions are unlikely to have the
effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In
reaching its decision, and based on the material currently before it,
the ACCC makes the following findings.
• The establishment and operation of the Proposed JV is subject
to the ACCC’s final determination on the Authorisation
Application, which is still under consideration.
• The Acquisitions are a necessary step in the establishment of
the Proposed JV but would not, without authorisation being
granted, result in any changes to the status quo in the
acquisition, supply and distribution of banknotes and coins in
Australia. In particular, the Major Banks will not cease
operating their individual cash pools, and the Proposed JV will
not replace Westpac as the sole supplier of commercial cash,
unless the ACCC separately grants authorisation for conduct
relating to the establishment and operation of JVCo and the
Joint Cash Pool.

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by
the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are
dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the
Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to
the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar
days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s
Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any
application for review, please contact the Australian Competition
Tribunal.
